questions.question: Which VPN model allows individual remote users to securely connect to a corporate intranet over the public internet?
options: Site-to-Site VPN
Remote-Access (client-to-site) VPN
Extranet VPN
Intranet-to-intranet VPN
questions.description: Remote-Access VPN (client-to-site) allows individual remote workers to connect to a corporate network via a VPN client. Site-to-site VPNs connect two fixed networks (branch offices), while extranet VPNs connect business partners.

questions.question: A 'Trusted VPN' fundamentally differs from a 'Secure VPN' because it:
options: Encrypts all data with AES-256
Relies on a trusted service provider's network path rather than encryption for security
Uses IPsec exclusively
Requires hardware-based VPN appliances at both ends
questions.description: A Trusted VPN (e.g., MPLS VPN) relies on the service provider guaranteeing a private, dedicated path through their network — no end-to-end encryption is assumed. A Secure VPN (e.g., IPsec, SSL) uses cryptographic protection over an untrusted network.

questions.question: Which VPN protocol operates at Layer 2 of the OSI model and is most commonly used for remote-access VPNs by tunneling PPP frames?
options: IPsec
L2TP
GRE
PPTP
questions.description: L2TP (Layer 2 Tunneling Protocol) operates at the Data Link layer and tunnels PPP frames. It is typically paired with IPsec (L2TP/IPsec) for encryption since L2TP itself provides no confidentiality. PPTP is older and considered insecure.

questions.question: The primary function of the VPN 'tunneling' mechanism is:
options: Encrypting data at rest on the VPN server
Encapsulating one network protocol inside another to traverse an incompatible or untrusted network
Filtering malicious traffic before it enters the corporate network
Assigning private IP addresses to remote clients
questions.description: Tunneling encapsulates packets of one protocol inside another — for example, private-addressed IP packets inside public-internet IP packets. The inner packet is 'hidden' from the transport network, creating a logical private path.

questions.question: SSL/TLS VPNs are preferred over IPsec VPNs for clientless remote access because:
options: SSL/TLS VPNs are faster due to hardware acceleration
SSL/TLS operates via standard web browsers without requiring dedicated VPN client software installation
SSL/TLS provides stronger encryption than IPsec
SSL/TLS VPNs support site-to-site connections better than IPsec
questions.description: SSL/TLS VPNs leverage the browser's built-in SSL/TLS stack, enabling clientless access to web applications through a portal. This avoids the complexity of deploying and managing IPsec client software on every user device.

questions.question: In a VPN, 'data integrity' is provided by which cryptographic mechanism?
options: Symmetric encryption (AES)
HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code)
Public key infrastructure (PKI)
Diffie-Hellman key exchange
questions.description: HMAC uses a shared secret key combined with a hash function (SHA-256, SHA-1) to produce a Message Authentication Code. If any bit of the data changes in transit, the HMAC verification fails, detecting tampering. Encryption provides confidentiality, not integrity verification.

questions.question: Which VPN characteristic ensures that even if long-term secret keys are compromised, past session keys cannot be decrypted?
options: Data confidentiality
Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)
Non-repudiation
Replay protection
questions.description: Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) uses ephemeral Diffie-Hellman key exchange to generate unique session keys that are never stored. Compromising the server's long-term private key does not allow decryption of past sessions because each session's key was independently derived and discarded.

questions.question: An Extranet VPN differs from an Intranet VPN in that it:
options: Uses stronger encryption
Connects an organization's internal sites to business partners, suppliers, or customers outside the organization
Operates exclusively over dedicated leased lines
Requires government-grade security clearance
questions.description: An Extranet VPN extends connectivity to external parties (partners, customers, suppliers) with controlled access to specific resources. An Intranet VPN connects geographically dispersed offices of the same organization using site-to-site tunnels.

questions.question: GRE (Generic Routing Encapsulation) tunnels are often combined with IPsec because:
options: GRE provides encryption; IPsec provides the tunnel
GRE provides the tunnel wrapper (supporting multicast/routing protocols); IPsec provides encryption and authentication for GRE packets
IPsec cannot support any tunneling without GRE
GRE compresses traffic before IPsec encrypts it
questions.description: GRE encapsulates multicast, routing protocol updates, and non-IP traffic, but provides no security. IPsec adds confidentiality, integrity, and authentication to the GRE tunnel. The combined GRE-over-IPsec solution is widely used in enterprise WAN designs.

questions.question: The Diffie-Hellman key exchange in VPN protocols is used specifically to:
options: Authenticate the VPN peer's identity
Establish a shared symmetric encryption key over an insecure channel without transmitting the key itself
Compress data before encryption
Generate digital certificates for VPN endpoints
questions.description: Diffie-Hellman allows two parties to independently compute the same shared secret using public values exchanged over an insecure network, without ever transmitting the actual key. This shared secret then seeds symmetric session key generation.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a core VPN function as defined by Stallings?
options: Confidentiality
Authentication
Integrity
Traffic shaping and QoS enforcement
questions.description: Core VPN functions are Confidentiality (encryption), Authentication (peer identity verification), Integrity (HMAC), and Replay protection (sequence numbers). QoS/traffic shaping is a network service, not a fundamental VPN security function.

questions.question: MPLS (Multiprotocol Label Switching) VPNs are classified as 'Trusted VPNs' because they:
options: Encrypt all customer traffic with AES-256
Create virtual private paths through the provider's network using label switching, with the provider guaranteeing isolation between customers
Require customers to install IPsec on all devices
Use dedicated physical circuits for each customer
questions.description: MPLS VPNs use label switching to route traffic through provider networks in logically isolated paths (VRFs — Virtual Routing and Forwarding instances). The 'trust' is in the provider's network isolation guarantee, not cryptographic protection of the customer's data.

questions.question: Replay attacks in VPNs are prevented by:
options: Using longer encryption keys
Including a sequence number in each packet and maintaining a sliding window to reject duplicate or out-of-order packets
Encrypting packet headers along with the payload
Using certificate-based authentication instead of pre-shared keys
questions.description: IPsec (and other VPN protocols) include a sequence number in each packet. The receiver maintains an anti-replay window — packets with sequence numbers already seen or outside the window are discarded, preventing attackers from recording and re-sending valid packets.

questions.question: In a SecureVPN, which authentication method provides the strongest mutual authentication for VPN peers?
options: Pre-shared keys (PSK)
Username and password
Digital certificates using PKI (X.509)
RADIUS server authentication
questions.description: X.509 digital certificates with PKI provide the strongest VPN peer authentication: each peer proves identity with a certificate signed by a trusted CA, providing mutual authentication without requiring shared secrets. PSK is simpler but requires secure out-of-band distribution of the same secret.

questions.question: In IPsec, what is the fundamental difference between Tunnel Mode and Transport Mode?
options: Tunnel mode encrypts only the payload; Transport mode encrypts the entire packet including the original IP header
Tunnel mode encapsulates the entire original IP packet inside a new IP header; Transport mode protects only the payload, leaving the original IP header intact
Transport mode is used for site-to-site VPNs; Tunnel mode is for remote access only
They use different encryption algorithms — AES for Tunnel, 3DES for Transport
questions.description: Tunnel Mode: the entire original IP packet (header + payload) is encapsulated inside a new IP packet — used for gateway-to-gateway VPNs. Transport Mode: only the payload is protected; the original IP header is retained — used for end-to-end host-to-host communication.

questions.question: IPsec's AH (Authentication Header) protocol provides which security services?
options: Confidentiality and authentication
Authentication and integrity only (no encryption)
Confidentiality only
Key exchange and digital signatures
questions.description: AH (Authentication Header) provides data origin authentication, connectionless integrity, and optional anti-replay protection — but NO confidentiality. For encryption, ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) must be used. AH authenticates the entire IP packet including the immutable header fields.

questions.question: The IPsec ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) protocol in Tunnel Mode protects against traffic analysis because:
options: It encrypts only the data payload, revealing source/destination IPs
It encrypts the entire original IP packet including the original source and destination addresses, hiding the real endpoint identities
It uses random padding to disguise packet sizes
It fragments all packets to the same size
questions.description: In ESP Tunnel Mode, the new outer IP header contains the VPN gateway addresses, while the original IP header (containing real endpoint addresses) is encrypted inside the ESP payload. An observer sees only gateway-to-gateway traffic, not the actual communicating hosts.

questions.question: IKE (Internet Key Exchange) Phase 1 in IPsec establishes:
options: The actual IPsec SAs for data protection
A secure authenticated channel (ISAKMP SA) used to negotiate Phase 2 SAs
The encryption keys for ESP directly
The routing tables for VPN traffic
questions.description: IKE Phase 1 establishes a secure, authenticated channel called the ISAKMP SA (or IKE SA) using Diffie-Hellman and authentication (certificates/PSK). This secure channel is then used in Phase 2 to negotiate the actual IPsec SAs (for ESP/AH) that protect user data.

questions.question: A 'Hybrid VPN' combines Secure VPN and Trusted VPN technologies. The primary motivation for this architecture is:
options: Cost reduction by eliminating dedicated hardware
To gain both the guaranteed path quality of MPLS and the end-to-end cryptographic security of IPsec/SSL over the provider's network
To bypass government encryption restrictions
To support both IPv4 and IPv6 simultaneously
questions.description: Hybrid VPN runs a Secure VPN (IPsec/SSL) over a Trusted VPN (MPLS). Organizations get MPLS QoS and path guarantees from the provider AND end-to-end encryption they control, so even if the provider's infrastructure is compromised, data remains protected.

questions.question: IPv6 has a unique relationship with IPsec because:
options: IPv6 is incompatible with IPsec and requires a separate tunneling protocol
IPsec support was mandated as a built-in requirement of the IPv6 specification (RFC 2460), though it is now optional in practice
IPv6 automatically encrypts all traffic without needing IPsec
IPv6 replaces IPsec with its own native security headers
questions.description: The original IPv6 specification (RFC 2460) mandated IPsec implementation. While later RFC 6434 changed this to a 'SHOULD' recommendation, IPv6 was designed with IPsec as a native security feature. IPv6 extension headers (AH, ESP) are part of the protocol design.

questions.question: In a Split-Tunnel VPN configuration, which traffic pattern applies?
options: All traffic (internet and corporate) is routed through the VPN tunnel
Only traffic destined for the corporate network is routed through the VPN; internet-bound traffic bypasses the tunnel and goes directly to the internet
Traffic is split between two different VPN protocols simultaneously
All DNS traffic is tunneled while HTTP traffic is sent directly
questions.description: Split tunneling routes only corporate-bound traffic through the VPN tunnel, while internet traffic exits directly through the local ISP. This reduces bandwidth on the corporate VPN but introduces risk: malware on the client can reach the internet without corporate security inspection.

questions.question: Full-tunnel VPN is preferred in enterprise environments primarily for which security reason?
options: It is faster than split tunneling
All internet traffic from remote workers passes through corporate security controls (firewall, proxy, DLP, content filtering)
It reduces the VPN server's processing load
It allows split DNS resolution
questions.description: Full-tunnel VPN forces ALL traffic (corporate + internet) through the VPN and corporate security stack. This ensures web filtering, DLP, and malware scanning apply to remote workers just as they would on-campus, at the cost of higher VPN bandwidth consumption.

questions.question: An IPsec Security Association (SA) is best defined as:
options: A mutual agreement between VPN peers to use the same encryption password
A one-way logical relationship between sender and receiver that defines the cryptographic parameters for a specific protected flow
A stateful firewall rule applied to VPN traffic
A PKI certificate binding a VPN gateway's identity to its public key
questions.description: An IPsec Security Association (SA) is a one-directional relationship specifying: the security protocol (AH/ESP), algorithm (AES-256/SHA-256), keys, and lifetime. Two SAs are needed for bidirectional communication. SAs are indexed by the SPI (Security Parameter Index).

questions.question: Which IPsec component maintains the list of active Security Associations and their parameters?
options: IKE daemon
Security Policy Database (SPD)
Security Association Database (SAD)
Certificate Revocation List (CRL)
questions.description: The Security Association Database (SAD) stores all active SAs — their SPI, destination IP, protocol (AH/ESP), keys, algorithms, and lifetimes. The Security Policy Database (SPD) contains policies defining what traffic should be protected, bypassed, or discarded.

questions.question: The fundamental operational difference between an IDS and an IPS is:
options: An IDS uses signatures; an IPS uses behavioral analysis
An IDS detects and alerts on suspicious activity; an IPS can actively block or prevent attacks in real time
An IDS is hardware-based; an IPS is software-based
An IDS monitors network traffic; an IPS monitors only host activities
questions.description: IDS (Intrusion Detection System) passively monitors traffic, generates alerts, but does not block traffic — it sits out-of-band. An IPS (Intrusion Prevention System) is deployed inline in the traffic path and can actively drop malicious packets, reset connections, or block traffic in real time.

questions.question: A 'vulnerability' in IDS/IPS context differs from a 'design flaw' in that:
options: Vulnerabilities are always exploitable; design flaws are theoretical
A vulnerability is a specific exploitable weakness in an implementation; a design flaw is an architectural/conceptual error in how a system was designed
Design flaws only exist in software; vulnerabilities exist in hardware
Vulnerabilities require physical access; design flaws can be exploited remotely
questions.description: Implementation vulnerability: a specific bug (buffer overflow, SQL injection) in code that can be exploited. Design flaw: a fundamental conceptual error (e.g., WEP's flawed IV reuse design) that makes the system inherently insecure regardless of correct implementation — the design itself is broken.

questions.question: Which tcpdump command captures only TCP traffic on port 443 from host 10.0.0.1 and saves to a file?
options: tcpdump tcp port 443 and host 10.0.0.1 -w capture.pcap
tcpdump -i eth0 'host 10.0.0.1 and tcp port 443' -w capture.pcap
tcpdump --filter 'tcp:443' --host 10.0.0.1 > capture.pcap
tcpdump capture -p 443 -h 10.0.0.1 -f capture.pcap
questions.description: tcpdump -i eth0 'host 10.0.0.1 and tcp port 443' -w capture.pcap — -i eth0 specifies interface, the BPF filter string uses boolean operators, and -w writes to a .pcap file. Always quote complex BPF expressions to prevent shell interpretation.

questions.question: A 'security event' in IDS terminology is best defined as:
options: Any network packet that traverses the monitored interface
Any observable occurrence in a network or system that may be relevant to security — may or may not indicate an actual attack
Only confirmed successful attacks
Firewall rule violations only
questions.description: A security event is any observable occurrence that may have security relevance — a login attempt, a port scan, a file access. Not all events are incidents. An incident is an event (or series of events) that actually violates security policy or causes harm.

questions.question: In the context of attack classification, a 'passive attack' differs from an 'active attack' in that:
options: Passive attacks require physical access; active attacks are remote
Passive attacks monitor or eavesdrop without modifying data; active attacks modify, fabricate, or disrupt data/services
Passive attacks use exploits; active attacks use social engineering
Passive attacks are always external; active attacks originate internally
questions.description: Passive attacks (traffic analysis, eavesdropping, sniffing) observe communications without altering them — difficult to detect. Active attacks (replay, modification, masquerade, DoS) involve modification, fabrication, or disruption — more visible but harder to prevent.

questions.question: Which tcpdump flag increases verbosity to display full packet content in both hex and ASCII?
options: -v
-vvv
-XX
-A
questions.description: -XX prints packet data in both hexadecimal and ASCII. -A prints only in ASCII (useful for HTTP). -vvv increases verbosity for protocol-level details. -X prints hex+ASCII for payload only; -XX includes the Ethernet header.

questions.question: A 'masquerade' attack in security terminology refers to:
options: Hiding malware inside legitimate files
An entity pretending to be a different, authorized entity to gain unauthorized access
Overloading a server with fake requests
Intercepting and modifying data in transit
questions.description: A masquerade attack involves one entity impersonating another — using stolen credentials, spoofed certificates, or session tokens — to gain privileges or access that would otherwise be denied. Authentication controls (MFA, certificates) are the primary countermeasure.

questions.question: The tcpdump BPF filter expression 'not port 22 and not host 192.168.1.1' captures:
options: All traffic on port 22 to/from 192.168.1.1
All traffic except SSH traffic and all traffic except traffic involving 192.168.1.1
All traffic to port 22 on any host except 192.168.1.1
Traffic on any port to 192.168.1.1, but not SSH
questions.description: BPF filter: not port 22 and not host 192.168.1.1 captures all traffic that is neither on port 22 nor involving host 192.168.1.1. Both conditions must be true (AND logic) — excludes port 22 traffic AND excludes 192.168.1.1 traffic.

questions.question: An IPS deployed 'inline' faces which significant operational risk compared to a passive IDS?
options: It generates more false negatives
If the IPS fails or malfunctions, it can become a single point of failure blocking all network traffic
It cannot detect encrypted traffic
It requires more storage for packet captures
questions.description: An inline IPS is in the network data path. Hardware failure, software crash, or misconfiguration can disrupt all traffic passing through it — creating a single point of failure. This requires redundancy (fail-open vs fail-closed design) and high-availability configurations.

questions.question: Which type of attack exploits a flaw in a vendor's software before the vendor has released a patch?
options: Replay attack
Zero-day exploit
Brute-force attack
Man-in-the-middle attack
questions.description: A zero-day exploit takes advantage of a previously unknown vulnerability — one that the software vendor has not yet had the opportunity to patch ('zero days' of warning). Zero-days are particularly dangerous because signature-based IDS/IPS cannot detect them until signatures are updated.

questions.question: Stallings classifies intruders into three categories. Which combination is correct?
options: Hackers, Crackers, Script Kiddies
Masqueraders, Misfeasors, Clandestine users
External attackers, Internal attackers, State-sponsored attackers
White-hat, Grey-hat, Black-hat
questions.description: Stallings' three intruder classes: Masquerader — outsider impersonating a legitimate user; Misfeasor — legitimate user abusing privileges (insider threat); Clandestine user — gains supervisory/admin control and uses it to evade audit/detection.

questions.question: A Network-Based IDS (NIDS) is best positioned at which network location for maximum visibility?
options: On each individual endpoint host
On the network segment between the firewall and internal network, monitoring traffic entering/leaving the protected zone
Inside the database server
On the VPN concentrator only
questions.description: A NIDS sensor should be placed where it can observe the most relevant traffic — typically at the network perimeter (after the firewall), at key internal segments, or in the DMZ. It passively copies and inspects traffic without disrupting the network.

questions.question: Anomaly-based IDS detection establishes a baseline of 'normal' behavior and alerts when deviations occur. The primary weakness of this approach is:
options: It cannot detect known attack signatures
High false-positive rate because legitimate but unusual activity may trigger alerts; also requires time to build an accurate baseline
It does not scale to high-bandwidth networks
It requires root access to all hosts
questions.description: Anomaly-based detection alerts on statistical deviations from baseline. The main challenges: (1) High false positives — legitimate new behaviors trigger alerts; (2) Baseline drift — if an attacker slowly changes normal behavior, the baseline adapts; (3) Requires weeks/months to establish accurate baselines.

questions.question: Signature-based IDS detection is most effective against which category of threats?
options: Zero-day exploits
Previously known and documented attacks for which signatures have been written
Slow, low-and-slow reconnaissance attacks
Encrypted command-and-control traffic
questions.description: Signature-based (misuse) detection compares traffic against a database of known attack patterns. It excels at detecting known attacks with very low false-positive rates. It completely fails against zero-days and novel attacks for which no signature exists.

questions.question: 'Defence in Depth' as a security architecture principle means:
options: Using the deepest available encryption
Implementing multiple layered security controls so that if one control fails, others continue to protect the asset
Deploying the most expensive security appliances
Focusing all security resources on perimeter defense
questions.description: Defence in Depth (also 'layered security') deploys multiple independent security controls across different layers — perimeter firewall, IPS, endpoint AV, application WAF, encryption, access controls. An attacker must defeat every layer; failure of any single control does not lead to total compromise.

questions.question: A distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attack differs from a traditional DoS attack in that:
options: DDoS targets only web servers; DoS targets any service
DDoS uses multiple coordinated compromised systems (botnet) to overwhelm the target simultaneously
DDoS uses application-layer attacks; DoS uses network-layer attacks only
DDoS requires physical access; DoS is entirely remote
questions.description: A DDoS attack orchestrates traffic from thousands/millions of compromised hosts (a botnet) simultaneously targeting a single victim. This massively amplifies the attack volume beyond what any single attacker's bandwidth could generate and makes source-blocking ineffective.

questions.question: The IDS analysis scheme 'alert correlation' addresses which specific problem?
options: Decrypting SSL traffic for inspection
Combining and analyzing multiple low-severity alerts to identify a coordinated attack that would be invisible when viewing events in isolation
Reducing network latency caused by IDS processing
Managing IDS signature updates automatically
questions.description: Alert correlation addresses the problem of alert flooding: a sophisticated attack generates thousands of individual low-level alerts. Correlation engines apply rules or ML to group related alerts, reconstruct attack sequences, and surface high-confidence incidents that no single alert reveals.

questions.question: The 'Threat Hunting' model differs from traditional IDS-based detection in that:
options: Threat hunting is fully automated; IDS requires human analysts
Threat hunting proactively searches for attacker presence in the environment using hypotheses, assuming the network may already be compromised; IDS reactively waits for rule/signature triggers
Threat hunting uses only open-source tools; IDS uses commercial products
Threat hunting only applies to cloud environments
questions.description: Threat Hunting is a proactive, hypothesis-driven approach where analysts actively search for signs of compromise, assuming attackers may already be present but undetected. Traditional IDS/SIEM is reactive — it triggers on known signatures or thresholds. Hunting catches attackers living off the land.

questions.question: Which IPS category is specifically designed to protect individual hosts and can monitor local application behavior and system calls?
options: Network-based IPS (NIPS)
Host-based IPS (HIPS)
Wireless IPS (WIPS)
Cloud-based IPS
questions.description: A Host-based IPS (HIPS) runs as an agent on individual endpoints, monitoring OS system calls, process behavior, registry changes, and local network connections. It can detect attacks that encrypt network traffic and prevent local privilege escalation even when the network IPS is blind.

questions.question: The 'principle of IDS' known as 'the intruder's behavior will be detectably different from that of a legitimate user' underpins which detection method?
options: Signature-based detection
Anomaly-based detection
Specification-based detection
Hybrid detection
questions.description: Anomaly-based detection is founded on the assumption that attacker behavior statistically deviates from established normal-user profiles. If this assumption holds, statistical outliers reveal intrusions. If attackers perfectly mimic normal behavior, anomaly detection fails — this is its fundamental limitation.

questions.question: An IDS generates a 'false negative'. This means:
options: The IDS incorrectly alerts on legitimate traffic
The IDS fails to detect a real attack — an intrusion occurs without triggering any alert
The IDS generates too many alerts for the analyst to process
The IDS correctly identifies and blocks an attack
questions.description: A false negative is the most dangerous IDS outcome — an actual attack occurs but the IDS does not detect it, giving a false sense of security. False positive: legitimate traffic triggers an alert. The balance between false positives and false negatives is the core IDS tuning challenge.

questions.question: In IDS context, a 'slow and low' attack is designed to evade detection by:
options: Using the highest possible bandwidth to overwhelm IDS sensors
Spreading attack activity over long periods at rates that fall below anomaly detection thresholds, blending with normal traffic
Encrypting all attack traffic with AES-256
Only attacking during maintenance windows
questions.description: Low-and-slow attacks deliberately limit their rate of activity (e.g., one port probe per hour, one login attempt per minute). This falls below thresholds that trigger rate-based anomaly detection and makes signature correlation across time windows extremely difficult.

questions.question: Specification-based IDS detection works by:
options: Comparing traffic against known attack signatures
Defining formal specifications of correct/expected behavior and alerting when actual behavior deviates from the specification
Building statistical profiles of normal traffic
Correlating alerts from multiple sensors
questions.description: Specification-based detection manually defines or automatically derives formal models of correct system/application behavior (state machines, protocol grammars). Any deviation from the spec triggers an alert. It has lower false-positive rates than pure anomaly detection and can catch zero-days if they violate specifications.

questions.question: Which IPS deployment mode allows the IPS to monitor a copy of traffic without being in the data path, preserving network availability?
options: Inline mode
Passive/tap mode (out-of-band)
Bridge mode
Transparent mode
questions.description: In passive/tap mode, the IPS receives a copy of traffic (via SPAN/mirror port or network TAP) and can only alert — it cannot block in real time. This eliminates the single-point-of-failure risk of inline deployment but sacrifices real-time prevention capability.

questions.question: The diamond model of intrusion analysis relates adversaries to victims through which two intermediate elements?
options: Vulnerability and exploit
Capability and infrastructure
Intent and opportunity
Signature and anomaly
questions.description: The Diamond Model connects: Adversary → uses Capability (tools, exploits, techniques) → deployed via Infrastructure (C2 servers, domains, IPs) → against Victim. This framework helps analysts pivot from one indicator to related intelligence about the full attack campaign.

questions.question: Which symptom in network traffic MOST strongly suggests an active reconnaissance phase preceding an attack?
options: Increased HTTP 200 responses from web servers
Sudden spike in ICMP echo requests, TCP SYN packets to multiple ports, or systematic DNS lookups across the IP range
High CPU usage on database servers
Large file transfers via SFTP
questions.description: Reconnaissance indicators include: systematic port scanning (TCP SYN to sequential ports), ICMP sweeps across subnets, DNS zone transfer attempts, and service fingerprinting. These precede exploitation and are detectable by IDS sensors monitoring for such patterns.

questions.question: A 'tiered IDS architecture' deploys sensors at multiple network levels. What is the primary benefit of this approach?
options: It reduces the total cost of IDS deployment
It provides correlated visibility across network perimeter, internal segments, and hosts — catching attacks that evade perimeter-only detection and enabling correlation across tiers
It simplifies management by centralizing all detection on one device
It eliminates false positives by cross-referencing all sensor feeds
questions.description: Tiered architecture deploys sensors at: (1) internet-facing perimeter, (2) internal network segments/DMZ, (3) host-based agents on critical servers. Attacks that bypass the perimeter sensor (e.g., internal lateral movement) are caught by internal or host sensors. The IDS manager correlates events across all tiers.

questions.question: A SYN flood DoS attack exploits which fundamental characteristic of the TCP three-way handshake?
options: TCP's use of sequence numbers for packet ordering
The server allocates resources (TCB — Transmission Control Block) for each half-open SYN received, which can be exhausted by flooding with SYN packets without completing the handshake
TCP's connection persistence after idle timeouts
The predictability of TCP initial sequence numbers
questions.description: In a SYN flood, the attacker sends thousands of SYN packets with spoofed source IPs. The server allocates a Transmission Control Block (TCB) for each and enters SYN_RCVD state, waiting for the final ACK that never comes. The server's connection queue fills up, denying service to legitimate connections.

questions.question: SYN cookies are a DoS mitigation technique that works by:
options: Blocking all SYN packets from unknown sources
Encoding connection state information into the ISN (initial sequence number) sent in the SYN-ACK, so the server doesn't allocate resources until the connection is completed with the ACK
Limiting the rate of SYN packets accepted per second
Requiring clients to solve a CAPTCHA before TCP connections are established
questions.description: SYN cookies encode the connection's state (src/dst IP, ports, timestamp) as a cryptographic hash in the SYN-ACK's initial sequence number. The server allocates no memory until it receives the ACK containing this value — proving the connection is legitimate. Spoofed SYNs never complete and consume no resources.

questions.question: DNS amplification DDoS attacks exploit which two characteristics of DNS?
options: DNS uses TCP and has large response times
DNS uses UDP (stateless, no handshake) and DNS responses can be much larger than queries — sending a small query to open resolvers that return large responses directed at the victim
DNS has no authentication mechanism
DNS relies on BGP for routing and can be hijacked
questions.description: DNS amplification exploits: (1) UDP statelessness — source IP can be spoofed to the victim's address; (2) Amplification factor — a 60-byte DNS ANY query to an open resolver can return a 3000-byte response (50x amplification), flooding the victim with traffic the attacker didn't generate.

questions.question: The primary difference between a network-based IDS sensor and a host-based IDS agent is:
options: Network sensors cost more than host agents
A network sensor inspects all traffic on a segment; a host agent monitors activity on a single host including system calls, file access, and local processes
Network sensors can only detect signature-based attacks; host agents use anomaly detection
Host agents require dedicated hardware; network sensors are software-only
questions.description: NIDS sensor: sits on the network, sees all traffic on the segment, cannot see inside encrypted packets, has no knowledge of host-level events. HIDS agent: runs on the host, sees plaintext application data, system calls, file integrity, and process activity — blind to network-level context of other hosts.

questions.question: An IDS 'agent' in a distributed IDS architecture performs which function?
options: Centrally stores and correlates all security events from all sensors
Collects local security events on a host or network segment, pre-processes them, and forwards relevant data to the IDS manager
Provides the user interface for analyst investigation
Automatically deploys countermeasures to block identified threats
questions.description: An IDS agent (or sensor agent) performs: local data collection (logs, network traffic, system calls), local analysis and filtering, and forwards relevant events/alerts to the central IDS manager. Local processing reduces bandwidth consumption to the manager.

questions.question: Which DDoS mitigation technique routes attack traffic to scrubbing centers where it is filtered before clean traffic is forwarded to the target?
options: Blackhole routing (null routing)
BGP-based traffic diversion to scrubbing centers (cloud-based DDoS mitigation)
SYN proxy/SYN cookie
Rate limiting at the edge router
questions.description: Cloud DDoS scrubbing services (e.g., Cloudflare, Akamai, AWS Shield) use BGP announcements to attract attack traffic to their globally distributed scrubbing infrastructure. Malicious traffic is filtered out; clean traffic is forwarded to the origin. This handles volumetric attacks exceeding any single organization's capacity.

questions.question: An IDS Manager in a distributed IDS architecture is responsible for:
options: Capturing packets on network interfaces
Receiving events from distributed agents/sensors, correlating alerts, maintaining the overall security picture, and providing analyst interface and response coordination
Installing and patching the IDS agents remotely
Encrypting all network traffic between monitored hosts
questions.description: The IDS Manager (or director) is the central component: receives alert data from all distributed agents/sensors, performs correlation across the enterprise, maintains the event database, generates consolidated reports, and provides the analyst console. It is the 'brain' of the distributed IDS.

questions.question: HTTP flood attacks are classified as which type of DoS, and why are they harder to mitigate than volumetric attacks?
options: Network-layer (Layer 3) attacks — harder because they consume all bandwidth
Application-layer (Layer 7) attacks — harder because they send seemingly legitimate HTTP requests that are indistinguishable from real traffic without deep inspection
Transport-layer (Layer 4) attacks — harder because they exploit TCP state tables
Physical-layer attacks — harder because they require on-site access
questions.description: HTTP flood is a Layer 7 application-layer attack sending valid HTTP GET/POST requests. Unlike volumetric attacks, each request is protocol-compliant and appears legitimate. Mitigation requires: JS challenges, CAPTCHA, behavioral analysis, rate limiting per session, and distinguishing bot from human traffic — far more complex than blocking spoofed IPs.

questions.question: A 'symptoms of attack' indicator called 'data exfiltration via DNS tunneling' is detected by IDS/sensors using which analysis method?
options: Checking DNS response sizes only
Analyzing DNS query frequency, unusually long subdomain labels, high entropy in query names, and large response payloads from non-standard resolvers
Blocking all DNS traffic except to known resolvers
Inspecting DNS packets only on port 53 UDP
questions.description: DNS tunneling encodes data in DNS subdomain names (e.g., base64-encoded data as subdomain labels). Detection signatures: abnormally long/random-looking subdomain names, high query rate to same domain, unusual record types (TXT, NULL), large responses. IDS rules analyze query entropy and statistical patterns.

questions.question: SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) combines two previously separate technologies. What are they?
options: Firewall and IDS
SIM (Security Information Management — log management/historical analysis) and SEM (Security Event Management — real-time monitoring/correlation)
Antivirus and network monitoring
Vulnerability scanner and patch management
questions.description: SIEM = SIM (Security Information Management): long-term log storage, compliance reporting, forensic analysis + SEM (Security Event Management): real-time event collection, correlation, alerting, and dashboards. Together they provide both real-time detection and historical forensic capability.

questions.question: SIEM 'log correlation' identifies security incidents by:
options: Encrypting all log data before storage
Applying rules and statistical analysis to find patterns across multiple log sources (firewall, IDS, AD, endpoints) that indicate an attack when individual events appear harmless in isolation
Deleting duplicate log entries to reduce storage
Forwarding all logs directly to law enforcement
questions.description: Log correlation connects seemingly unrelated events: e.g., a failed VPN login (firewall log) + successful login 10 minutes later from a different country (AD log) + access to sensitive files (DLP log) = credential compromise alert. No single event triggers an alert, but the pattern does.

questions.question: The common log forwarding protocol used to send logs from network devices to a SIEM is:
options: SNMP traps
Syslog (UDP/514 or TCP/514 or TLS/6514)
NetFlow v9
ICMP-based reporting
questions.description: Syslog (RFC 5424) is the standard protocol for forwarding log messages from routers, firewalls, servers, and applications to a central log server or SIEM. Port 514 UDP is traditional (unreliable), 514 TCP provides reliability, and 6514 TCP uses TLS for encrypted log transmission.

questions.question: A SIEM 'event trigger' (correlation rule) fires when 5 failed SSH login attempts from the same IP occur within 60 seconds, followed by a successful login. This rule is designed to detect:
options: SSH port scanning
Brute-force attack followed by successful compromise — a high-fidelity composite indicator
Legitimate users with forgotten passwords
SSH key rotation events
questions.description: This composite rule detects brute-force followed by successful compromise: N failures (pattern of guessing) + 1 success strongly indicates the attacker found valid credentials. Individual events are ambiguous; the correlation (failures → success) creates a high-confidence security incident.

questions.question: In SIEM architecture, 'log normalization' is critical because:
options: It compresses logs to save storage space

Different devices use different log formats (Windows Event Log, syslog, CEF, LEEF) — normalization converts all formats into a common schema so correlation rules can be applied uniformly across all sources
It encrypts logs to prevent unauthorized access
It deletes low-severity events to reduce noise
questions.description: Log normalization maps diverse, vendor-specific log formats into a common event schema (e.g., all 'source IP' fields mapped to a single normalized field). Without normalization, correlation rules would need to account for every vendor's unique field naming, making cross-source correlation impossible.

questions.question: Which SIEM capability uses statistical baselines to detect anomalous user behavior such as data access patterns, login times, and file modifications?
options: Rule-based correlation
UEBA (User and Entity Behavior Analytics)
Log normalization
Threat intelligence feed integration
questions.description: UEBA (User and Entity Behavior Analytics) uses ML/statistical modeling to establish baselines of normal behavior for each user/entity. Deviations — e.g., a user downloading 10x more data than usual at 3 AM, or accessing systems they've never touched — trigger alerts for insider threats and compromised accounts.

questions.question: In Snort's rule syntax, what does the following rule option 'content:"GET /admin"; nocase;' specify?
options: Match only exact uppercase 'GET /admin' in the packet payload
Match the string 'GET /admin' in the packet payload in a case-insensitive manner
Match the packet if it does NOT contain 'GET /admin'
Check only the HTTP header for 'GET /admin' regardless of case
questions.description: The Snort content keyword searches the packet payload for the specified string. nocase makes the match case-insensitive — matching 'get /admin', 'GET /ADMIN', 'Get /Admin', etc. Without nocase, content matching is case-sensitive by default.

questions.question: An attacker bypasses a Snort IDS by fragmenting malicious payload across multiple TCP segments set to arrive in the wrong order. This IDS evasion technique is called:
options: Protocol tunneling
TCP/IP session splicing and fragmentation evasion
Polymorphic shellcode
Time-to-live (TTL) manipulation
questions.description: Session splicing splits the malicious payload across multiple small TCP segments. If the IDS doesn't perform full TCP stream reassembly before pattern matching, it sees only fragments — no single segment contains the complete signature. The target host reassembles and executes the payload. The fix is full reassembly in the IDS.

questions.question: Snort operates in three modes. Which mode actively drops packets matching rules in addition to generating alerts?
options: Sniffer mode
Packet logger mode
Inline/IPS mode
Passive detection mode
questions.description: Inline/IPS mode (using DAQ — Data Acquisition library with iptables/NFQ) allows Snort to DROP, REJECT, or PASS packets in real time based on rules. Sniffer mode displays packets on-screen; Packet logger mode saves packets to disk; both are passive (detection only, no blocking).

questions.question: A 'polymorphic' attack evades signature-based IDS by:
options: Sending traffic faster than the IDS can process
Changing the attack's code or payload encoding with each iteration while preserving its functional behavior, making fixed byte-sequence signatures ineffective
Fragmenting packets below the IDS minimum reassembly size
Using encrypted channels to hide attack traffic
questions.description: Polymorphic attacks use mutation engines to rewrite exploit shellcode (different XOR keys, different encodings, NOP sled variations) so each instance looks different in bytes. Signature-based IDS rules that match specific byte sequences fail — each sample is unique while the functional exploit remains. Heuristic/behavioral detection is needed.
